content.problemStatement:
There is a tree with NNN nodes, numbered from 1 to NNN. The tree is rooted at node 1. The tree represents the structure of a big mafia syndicate, and each node represents a criminal. Each criminal, except the leader (who is denoted by node 1), is under the supervision of his boss, who is his parent in the tree.

Initially, each criminal/node except the leader has some money. Starting now, at every second, each criminal will pass on all the money that he has, to his boss (i.e. parent). And during the same second, his 'children' might have passed on some amount to him, which he will pass on to his parent in the next second, and so on.

However, a criminal might go rogue if the money he has strictly exceeds some threshold (that is, if the money that he has is greater than a particular value, he is overpowered by greed). Going rogue means, at some point, if the amount of money that he has to pass on to his boss is greater than the threshold, he won't do so, and instead keep the amount to himself. And after this, all the money that comes to him, he will keep to himself. He will not pass on anything after this.

So, as an example if there is a tree, 1 - 2 - 3, in which 1 is the root, 2 is beneath 1, and 3 is beneath 2. And suppose the amount of money that each of the criminals initially have is (Note that the root node will not have anything in the beginning):

have[1]=0have[1] = 0have[1]=0

have[2]=10have[2] = 10have[2]=10

have[3]=100have[3] = 100have[3]=100

And their thresholds are:

cap[2]=10cap[2] = 10cap[2]=10

cap[3]=1000cap[3] = 1000cap[3]=1000

That is, if at any second, node 2 has strictly more than 10 amount of money, then he will turn rogue, and will stop passing the money to his boss.

Now, at the first second, no criminal has more money than his threshold. So, both criminals 2 and 3 pass on their money to their bosses. Thus, after the first second, the criminals will have the following:

have[1]=10have[1] = 10have[1]=10

have[2]=100have[2] = 100have[2]=100

have[3]=0have[3] = 0have[3]=0

But now, since have[2]>cap[2]have[2] \gt cap[2]have[2]>cap[2], criminal 2 turns rogue, and won't pass on any money to his boss anymore. Nothing changes after this, and so this is the stable state.

You can show that after some finite amount of time, the configuration remains constant, with no more passing of money. We call this the stable state. The problem is, given the tree with NNN nodes, and two arrays have[2],have[3],…,have[N]have[2], have[3], \ldots, have[N]have[2],have[3],…,have[N] and cap[2],cap[3],…,cap[N]cap[2], cap[3], \ldots, cap[N]cap[2],cap[3],…,cap[N] of size N−1N - 1N−1, find out the total money which the leader (ie. root node) will have at the end, in the stable state.


Input Format

The first line contains the integer NNN, denoting the number of criminals/nodes in the syndicate/tree.
The ithi^{th}ith of the next N−1N - 1N−1 lines contains two space separated integers, have[i+1]have[i+1]have[i+1] and cap[i+1]cap[i+1]cap[i+1], which correspond to the amount that criminal i+1i+1i+1 initally has, and the threshold beyond which he will turn rogue. Note that have[1]have[1]have[1] is 0 and cap[1]cap[1]cap[1] is irrelevant, so they are not provided in the input.
The ithi^{th}ith of the next N−1N - 1N−1 lines contains a single integer, denoting the immediate boss of the (i+1)th(i+1)^{th}(i+1)th criminal. Note that criminal 111 (i.e. the leader) has no immediate boss, and hence is not included in this input.


Output Format
Print the total amount present at the root node at the end, in the stable state.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains the integer N, denoting the number of criminals/nodes in the syndicate/tree. The ith of the next N-1 lines contains two space-separated integers, have[i+1] and cap[i+1], which correspond to the amount that criminal i+1 initially has and the threshold beyond which he will turn rogue. The ith of the next N-1 lines contains a single integer, denoting the immediate boss of the (i+1)th criminal.
content.outputFormat: The total amount present at the root node at the end, in the stable state.

sampleTestCases.explanation: The root node receives 10 units of money from node 2 in the first second. Node 2 turns rogue in the next second and stops passing money to the root node.

The root node receives 10 units of money from node 2 in the first second. Node 2 turns rogue in the next second and stops passing money to the root node. Node 3 passes 1 unit of money to node 2 in the second second, which is then passed to the root node.

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we can use a tree data structure to represent the mafia syndicate. We start by initializing the tree with the given nodes and their corresponding have and cap values. Then, we perform a BFS traversal of the tree, where at each level, we update the have values of the nodes based on the amount of money they receive from their children and the amount they pass on to their parent. If a node's have value exceeds its cap value, it turns rogue and stops passing money to its parent. We continue this process until no more money is being passed between nodes, at which point we have reached the stable state. The total amount present at the root node at the end is the answer.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N) — where N is the number of nodes in the tree. This is because we perform a constant amount of work for each node in the tree.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N) — where N is the number of nodes in the tree. This is because we need to store the tree data structure in memory.

code.Python3:
# COMPLETE, RUNNABLE Python3 code
from collections import deque

# Define the structure for a tree node
class Node:
    def __init__(self, have, cap):
        self.have = have
        self.cap = cap
        self.parent = -1
        self.rogue = False

# Function to perform BFS traversal of the tree
def bfs(nodes, bosses, N):
    q = deque([0])

    while q:
        node_index = q.popleft()
        node = nodes[node_index]

        # Update the have values of the node's children
        for i in range(N):
            if bosses[i] == node_index:
                child = nodes[i]
                if not child.rogue:
                    node.have += child.have
                    child.have = 0

        # Check if the node turns rogue
        if node.have > node.cap:
            node.rogue = True

        # Add the node's parent to the queue
        if node.parent != -1:
            q.append(node.parent)

# Read the input
N = int(input())

nodes = [None] * N
bosses = [0] * (N - 1)

# Read the have and cap values for each node
for i in range(1, N):
    have, cap = map(int, input().split())
    nodes[i] = Node(have, cap)

# Read the boss for each node
for i in range(1, N):
    bosses[i - 1] = int(input())
    nodes[i].parent = bosses[i - 1]

# Perform BFS traversal of the tree
bfs(nodes, bosses, N)

# Print the total amount present at the root node
print(nodes[0].have)
